stem: A soccer player plants and pivots, feels a "pop," and the knee swells within 2 hours. Which findings/tests would support an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear? Select all that apply.
choices: Positive anterior drawer of the knee
rationale: Lachman (most sensitive), anterior drawer, and pivot shift all assess anterior tibial translation/rotatory instability from ACL deficiency. McMurray assesses the meniscus; Thompson assesses Achilles continuity — neither evaluates the ACL.

stem: Per the Ottawa Ankle Rules, radiographs of the ankle are indicated when there is pain in the malleolar zone AND which of the following? Select all that apply.
choices: Bony tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of the lateral malleolus
Bony tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of the medial malleolus
Inability to bear weight for 4 steps both immediately and in the clinic
Visible swelling over the anterior talofibular ligament
A positive anterior drawer of the ankle
rationale: The Ottawa Ankle Rules image when malleolar-zone pain is accompanied by bony tenderness at the posterior edge/tip of either malleolus OR inability to bear weight 4 steps immediately and in the clinic. Soft-tissue swelling and ligamentous laxity (anterior drawer) are not part of the rule.

stem: Which single special test is the MOST sensitive for an anterior cruciate ligament tear?
rationale: The Lachman test (anterior tibial translation at 20–30° flexion) is the most sensitive ACL test; the pivot shift is the most specific. Valgus stress assesses the MCL and McMurray the meniscus.

stem: A swimmer reports anterior shoulder pain with overhead motion. Which findings would support subacromial impingement / rotator-cuff pathology? Select all that apply.
rationale: Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer reproduce subacromial impingement; the empty-can isolates the supraspinatus; a positive drop-arm suggests a rotator-cuff (supraspinatus) tear. Spurling assesses cervical radiculopathy, not the shoulder.

stem: An athlete has neck and radiating arm pain after a stinger. Spurling's test reproduces radicular symptoms down the arm. This finding most directly supports which clinical diagnosis?
rationale: Spurling's (extension + rotation + axial compression toward the symptomatic side) narrows the neural foramen and reproduces radicular pain in cervical radiculopathy. It does not implicate the AC joint, glenohumeral capsule, or subacromial bursa.

stem: Which assessment tools are appropriate for evaluating a suspected sport-related concussion? Select all that apply.
rationale: SCAT6, VOMS, BESS, and King-Devick all assess concussion-related domains (symptoms, vestibular/ocular function, balance, rapid number naming). The Ottawa Ankle Rules are an ankle-imaging decision tool, unrelated to concussion.

stem: The anterior drawer test of the ANKLE primarily assesses the integrity of which structure?
rationale: The ankle anterior drawer stresses the ATFL (the most commonly injured lateral ligament). The talar tilt assesses the CFL; the squeeze/external-rotation tests assess the syndesmosis; Thompson assesses the Achilles.

stem: A runner reports deep, exercise-induced leg pain with numbness that resolves with rest. Which findings should raise concern for chronic exertional compartment syndrome and prompt physician referral? Select all that apply.
choices: Pain that predictably begins at a set exertion level and resolves with rest
Paresthesia in the distribution of a compartment's nerve
Immediate relief with stretching the involved muscle
rationale: Reproducible exertional pain, post-exertion compartment tightness, nerve-distribution paresthesia, and elevated intracompartmental pressures characterize chronic exertional compartment syndrome. Symptoms are not relieved by stretching — that finding points away from the diagnosis.

stem: When taking a focused history (the H in HOPS / the S in SOAP), which items are appropriate to elicit? Select all that apply.
rationale: History is subjective: mechanism, symptom location/behavior, prior injury, and aggravating/easing factors. Goniometric ROM is an objective, measured finding obtained during the physical exam, not the history.

stem: A wrestler lands on a flexed, adducted hip and cannot bear weight; the limb appears shortened, flexed, adducted, and internally rotated. The most likely clinical diagnosis is:
rationale: A posterior hip dislocation classically presents with the limb shortened, flexed, adducted, and internally rotated — a medical emergency requiring immobilization and urgent transport (risk to the femoral head blood supply and sciatic nerve).

stem: An athlete collapses during football in a WBGT of 90°F with confusion and a rectal temperature of 105.8°F. Which actions are appropriate in immediate management? Select all that apply.
choices: Begin cold-water immersion on-site before transport (cool first, transport second)
Monitor core temperature with a rectal thermistor during cooling
Delay cooling until the athlete reaches the emergency department
Stop cooling when rectal temperature reaches approximately 102°F
rationale: Exertional heat stroke is treated cool-first/transport-second with cold-water immersion, continuous rectal-temperature monitoring, EMS activation, and cooling to ~102°F before stopping. Delaying cooling until the ED increases morbidity/mortality.

stem: Which body site provides the ONLY accurate measure of core temperature in a collapsed, exercising athlete with suspected exertional heat stroke?
rationale: Only rectal temperature is valid in this setting; oral, tympanic, axillary, and temporal readings are unreliable in a sweating, exercising, or dehydrated athlete and can falsely lower the reading.

stem: A bee sting triggers hives, lip swelling, and wheezing in an athlete with known severe allergy. Which actions are appropriate in immediate management of anaphylaxis? Select all that apply.
choices: Administer intramuscular epinephrine to the mid-outer thigh
Position the patient and support the airway; give supplemental oxygen if available
Be prepared to give a second epinephrine dose if symptoms persist
Withhold epinephrine and give only an oral antihistamine
rationale: Anaphylaxis is treated with prompt IM epinephrine (vastus lateralis), EMS activation, airway/oxygen support, and a repeat dose if needed. Antihistamines are adjuncts only and never replace epinephrine.

stem: An adult athlete is unresponsive with no normal breathing. After activating EMS and sending for an AED, the next immediate step is:
choices: Begin chest compressions at 100–120/min, 2–2.4 inches deep
Check the pulse for a full 30 seconds before acting
Wait for the AED to arrive before starting CPR
Place the athlete in the recovery position
rationale: Start high-quality chest compressions immediately; a pulse check should take no more than 10 seconds, and compressions should not be delayed for the AED or for breaths in adult sudden cardiac arrest.

stem: A venue-specific Emergency Action Plan (EAP) should include which required elements? Select all that apply.
choices: Assigned personnel roles (caller, scene manager, equipment retriever, EMS escort)
Primary and backup communication methods and the 911/EMS contact
Location of emergency equipment, including the AED
Venue-specific EMS access routes, gate codes, and a map
The team's seasonal strength-and-conditioning periodization plan
rationale: An EAP defines personnel roles, communication, equipment locations, and venue-specific EMS access — and should be written, rehearsed, and reviewed annually. A conditioning periodization plan is unrelated to emergency response.

stem: An equipped football player is supine with a suspected cervical-spine injury and is breathing normally. Which immediate actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
choices: If airway access is needed, remove the face mask while leaving the helmet and shoulder pads in place
Routinely remove the helmet immediately while leaving shoulder pads on
Log-roll only with adequate trained personnel and cervical control if the patient must be moved or repositioned
rationale: Maintain in-line stabilization, activate EMS, and gain airway access by removing the face mask while keeping helmet and shoulder pads together (they keep the head/spine aligned). Removing the helmet alone leaves the head extended on the pads and is not routine; movement requires trained personnel with cervical control.
